Eliane, I read your draft. Before sending it, I recommend cutting the first three pages.
I agree, Marcus. I decided to open with a one-page executive summary instead.
Good. And the value proposition? Investors want to see one clear reason to choose you.
It is one line. We offer a technology that lowers the cost per cycle, so patients pay less.
That is worth stating first. Now, the numbers. When do you plan to reach break-even?
In month eighteen. The forecast assumes a slow first year, so I avoid overpromising.
Smart. Did you remember to add a contingency for the tight months?
Yes. And before writing each deliverable, I tested its feasibility with the team.
Then you are ready. The plan that wins is the one that is clear enough to trust.
I recommend opening with a tight executive summary.
We decided to fund a slow first year.
It is worth adding a contingency for the tight months.
We aim to reach break-even in month eighteen.
Before writing a promise, I test its feasibility.

Hi Eliane, it is Marcus, your advisor. Before you send that proposal, let me give you one piece of advice: investors decide to keep reading, or to stop, on the first page. So I recommend opening with a tight executive summary, no more than one paragraph. Avoid promising everything. Choose one value proposition and defend it. In the financial section, be honest: show a realistic projection, keep the forecast modest for the first year, and say clearly when you reach break-even. It is worth adding a contingency, because a plan that hides its risk is a plan no one trusts. And before you write a single promise, test its feasibility, ask whether the team can really deliver it and whether it is cost-effective to run. Then streamline everything. Cut any page that does not help an investor decide. Keep it clear, keep it viable, and let the numbers do the persuading.

Hi team, it is Eliane. Let me recap where the proposal stands so we can finish it together. I have decided to open with a one-page executive summary, because Marcus keeps reminding me that investors stop reading fast. Our value proposition is simple: we offer one thing the incumbent does not. In the numbers, I want to avoid overpromising, so the forecast assumes a slow start and the projection reaches break-even in month eighteen. I recommend adding a contingency for the tight months, and I plan to test the feasibility of every deliverable before we write it down. Our job this week is to streamline the whole document, cutting anything that does not help a reader decide. If the plan is clear, honest and cost-effective, it proves the idea is viable. That is the plan that wins backing, and that is the one we are going to send.
